panel.origin: Thin, wispy, feathery streaks high in the sky — the highest of the common clouds, made entirely of ice crystals.
panel.chemistry: Forms above ~6,000 m where the air is freezing cold. Ice crystals fall and get swept sideways by high winds into those delicate 'mare's tails.'
panel.mixology: Often the first sign that a warm front and rain are approaching within a day or so. On their own, they mean fair but changing weather.
panel.examples: Delicate white filaments or hooks, very high up, that don't block the sun. No shading underneath.

panel.origin: A thin, milky veil of high cloud that covers the whole sky and often produces a halo around the sun or moon.
panel.chemistry: A sheet of ice crystals spreading ahead of an advancing warm front, thin enough that the sun still shines through.
panel.mixology: A reliable sign rain or snow is likely within 12–24 hours as the front approaches.
panel.examples: Whitish sky-wide haze with a ring (halo) around the sun or moon — the giveaway feature.

panel.origin: Small, white, rippled patches high in the sky — the classic 'mackerel sky' that looks like fish scales.
panel.chemistry: Ice crystals arranged in tiny regular cells by gentle turbulence at high altitude. Short-lived, often changing into other cloud types.
panel.mixology: Folklore says 'mackerel sky, never long dry' — often a sign of changing, unsettled weather ahead.
panel.examples: Rows of tiny white ripples or grains, no shading, very high. Like fish scales across the sky.

panel.origin: The straight white lines aircraft leave behind — artificial clouds made of ice crystals from engine exhaust.
panel.chemistry: Hot, moist jet exhaust meets very cold high-altitude air and the water vapor freezes instantly into a line of ice crystals.
panel.mixology: How long they last hints at high-altitude humidity: short-lived means dry air, long-lasting and spreading means moist air and possible weather change.
panel.examples: Perfectly straight lines following flight paths, unlike any natural cloud. Spreading ones can blur into cirrus.

panel.origin: A gray or blue-gray sheet covering much of the sky, through which the sun appears as a dim, watery disc.
panel.chemistry: A uniform mid-level layer of water droplets and ice, thickening ahead of a warm front or large weather system.
panel.mixology: Steady, widespread rain or snow often follows within hours as it thickens and lowers into nimbostratus.
panel.examples: The 'frosted glass' look — sun visible as a faint glow with no halo and no sharp shadows on the ground.

panel.origin: Mid-level patches or rolls of white and gray cloud, often in groups or waves across part of the sky.
panel.chemistry: Water droplets organized into cells by convection and wind at medium height, frequently in distinctive rows.
panel.mixology: On a warm, humid morning, altocumulus can warn of afternoon thunderstorms. Otherwise it signals fair but unsettled air.
panel.examples: Mid-sized cloud blobs in groups, each with some shading, often in rippled bands. Bigger than cirrocumulus, higher than stratocumulus.

panel.origin: Smooth, lens- or saucer-shaped clouds that hover near mountains — the ones often mistaken for UFOs.
panel.chemistry: Moist air flowing over a mountain rises and cools in a standing wave, condensing into a stationary lens shape at the wave's crest.
panel.mixology: Indicate strong winds and atmospheric waves aloft — a warning sign for pilots of turbulence near mountains.
panel.examples: A stack of smooth, stationary 'flying saucer' discs downwind of a mountain peak, holding still while other clouds move.

panel.origin: A low, gray, featureless blanket covering the whole sky — essentially fog that didn't touch the ground.
panel.chemistry: A shallow layer of cloud forms when a large body of cool, moist air is lifted gently or mist rises slightly off the surface.
panel.mixology: Means dull, overcast, often drizzly weather. Usually no heavy rain, just gloom and possible mist.
panel.examples: Flat, uniform gray with no distinct shapes and no visible sun. Lowest of the layered clouds.

panel.origin: Low, lumpy gray-and-white cloud arranged in patches, rolls, or a broken honeycomb across the sky.
panel.chemistry: A low layer broken into cells by weak convection — the most common cloud type on Earth, especially over oceans.
panel.mixology: Usually means dry, settled weather; rarely produces more than light drizzle. Often clears by afternoon.
panel.examples: Low, clumpy rolls with darker bases and gaps of blue or lighter sky between them.

panel.origin: The classic puffy 'cotton ball' or 'cauliflower' cloud with a flat base and bright white rounded top.
panel.chemistry: Rising bubbles of warm air (thermals) cool as they ascend until their moisture condenses, building a fluffy heap.
panel.mixology: Small, scattered cumulus on a sunny day means fair weather. If they grow tall through the afternoon, storms may follow.
panel.examples: Detached white puffs with flat gray bottoms and bright bumpy tops, drifting across blue sky.

panel.origin: Cloud at ground level — a suspension of tiny water droplets that cuts visibility to under a kilometer.
panel.chemistry: Forms when moist air cools to its dew point near the surface: overnight cooling (radiation fog) or warm air over a cold surface (advection fog).
panel.mixology: Signals very humid, calm conditions. Usually burns off as the sun warms the ground, but can be a serious driving hazard.
panel.examples: A gray veil at eye level reducing visibility, often densest in valleys and near water at dawn.
panel.flag: Dense fog is a major driving and aviation hazard — slow down and use low-beam lights.

panel.origin: The thick, dark gray rain cloud — a featureless, sky-covering layer that delivers steady, prolonged precipitation.
panel.chemistry: A deep, moisture-laden layer that thickens and lowers from altostratus as a warm front arrives, raining continuously.
panel.mixology: Means hours of steady, soaking rain or snow — not the brief downpour of a thunderstorm, but the all-day kind.
panel.examples: Uniformly dark, low, and wet-looking, with no structure and falling rain or snow blurring its base.

panel.origin: The king of clouds — a towering thundercloud that can reach the top of the troposphere, bringing storms.
panel.chemistry: A cumulus cloud fed by strong updrafts grows explosively upward until it hits the stratosphere and flattens into an anvil.
panel.mixology: Means imminent severe weather: heavy rain, lightning, hail, strong winds, and possibly tornadoes.
panel.examples: A huge, dark-based tower with a flat, spreading 'anvil' top. Often the only cloud that matters when you see one.
panel.flag: Cumulonimbus signals lightning and severe weather — seek shelter; do not stay in open or high ground.

panel.origin: A cumulus cloud that has grown tall and cauliflower-like — the stage just before it becomes a thunderstorm.
panel.chemistry: Vigorous updrafts push a fair-weather cumulus sharply upward, its top hardening into firm, sprouting domes.
panel.mixology: A warning that the atmosphere is unstable and full thunderstorms (cumulonimbus) may develop within the hour.
panel.examples: Cumulus that's clearly taller than it is wide, with crisp, surging tops but not yet an anvil.

panel.origin: Strange pouches or lobes hanging down from the underside of a cloud, usually a thunderstorm's anvil.
panel.chemistry: Pockets of cooler, sinking air sag below the cloud base, forming the distinctive drooping bubbles.
panel.mixology: Often appear with or after severe thunderstorms — a sign of a turbulent, energetic storm nearby.
panel.examples: Rows of smooth, hanging pouches under a dark cloud, often lit dramatically near sunset.

panel.origin: A low, wedge-shaped cloud on the leading edge of a thunderstorm, looking like an advancing shelf or ledge.
panel.chemistry: Cool air rushing out ahead of a storm (the outflow) lifts warm moist air, condensing it into a long horizontal wedge.
panel.mixology: Marks the gust front — strong, sudden winds and heavy rain arrive right behind it.
panel.examples: A long, low, smooth horizontal ledge stretching across the sky ahead of a dark storm, often rising and ragged underneath.

panel.origin: Liquid water droplets large enough to fall from cloud to ground — the most common form of precipitation.
panel.chemistry: Cloud droplets collide and merge (or melt from ice) until they're heavy enough to overcome the updraft and fall.
panel.mixology: The type and intensity reveal the weather system: steady rain from fronts, sudden downpours from convective storms.
panel.examples: Falling drops you can see and feel; steady from layered cloud, bursty from towering cloud.

panel.origin: Ice crystals that fall as flakes when the whole atmosphere is below freezing.
panel.chemistry: Water vapor freezes directly onto ice crystals in the cloud, which clump into flakes and fall without melting.
panel.mixology: Tells you the air column is at or below freezing top to bottom. Wet snow is near 0°C; powder is much colder.
panel.examples: White flakes; large and sticky when near freezing, small and dry/powdery when very cold.

panel.origin: Balls or lumps of ice that fall from strong thunderstorms, ranging from pea-sized to grapefruit-sized.
panel.chemistry: Ice is carried up and down repeatedly by violent updrafts, gaining a new frozen layer each trip until too heavy to hold aloft.
panel.mixology: A marker of a powerful storm with strong updrafts. Large hail signals a severe, possibly tornadic storm.
panel.examples: Solid ice pellets bouncing on the ground, often with visible layers if you cut one open.
panel.flag: Large hail is dangerous and damaging — shelter indoors away from windows.

panel.origin: Small, translucent ice pellets that bounce when they hit the ground — frozen raindrops.
panel.chemistry: Snow melts falling through a warm layer, then refreezes into ice pellets passing through cold air near the surface.
panel.mixology: Indicates a shallow warm layer aloft over freezing air below — a classic winter-storm temperature profile.
panel.examples: Tiny clear ice balls that bounce and rattle on hard surfaces, unlike soft snow.

panel.origin: Rain that falls as liquid but freezes on contact with cold surfaces, coating everything in clear ice.
panel.chemistry: Snow melts in a warm layer and the raindrops stay liquid but supercooled, freezing instantly when they touch a sub-freezing surface.
panel.mixology: The most dangerous winter precipitation — it glazes roads, trees, and power lines with ice.
panel.examples: Wet-looking rain that turns into a smooth, clear ice coating on cars, branches, and pavement.
panel.flag: Freezing rain creates extremely hazardous black ice and downs power lines — avoid travel during ice storms.

panel.origin: Very fine, light rain made of tiny droplets that seem to float down slowly.
panel.chemistry: Falls from low stratus cloud where droplets are too small to grow into proper raindrops.
panel.mixology: Signals a moist, stable, overcast airmass — gentle and persistent rather than heavy.
panel.examples: Mist-like droplets so fine they barely make a sound, often with low gray cloud or fog.

panel.origin: A storm from a cumulonimbus cloud, producing lightning, thunder, heavy rain, and often gusty wind.
panel.chemistry: Strong updrafts build a tall thundercloud; charge separates within it until it discharges as lightning, heating air into thunder.
panel.mixology: Means heavy rain, lightning danger, and possibly hail or damaging wind for a short, intense period.
panel.examples: Dark towering cloud, rumbling thunder, sudden heavy rain and wind; lightning flashes the giveaway.
panel.flag: When thunder roars, go indoors — lightning can strike well away from the rain.

panel.origin: The most dangerous kind of thunderstorm, organized around a deep, rotating updraft called a mesocyclone.
panel.chemistry: Wind that changes speed and direction with height tilts the updraft into a long-lived rotating column, sustaining the storm for hours.
panel.mixology: Produces the most violent weather: giant hail, destructive winds, and the majority of strong tornadoes.
panel.examples: A single dominant storm, often with a rotating wall cloud, a hard 'overshooting' top, and a striated, sculpted look.
panel.flag: Supercells are the primary tornado producers — follow warnings and shelter immediately.

panel.origin: A violently rotating column of air reaching from a thunderstorm cloud down to the ground.
panel.chemistry: A storm's rotating mesocyclone tightens and stretches toward the surface, accelerating the spin into a destructive vortex.
panel.mixology: Among the most destructive weather on Earth, with winds that can exceed 300 km/h in the strongest cases.
panel.examples: A funnel or rope of cloud and debris connecting cloud to ground, often with a roaring sound and dark, rotating sky.
panel.flag: Tornadoes are deadly — go to a basement or interior room on the lowest floor, away from windows, at once.

panel.origin: A massive rotating tropical storm with sustained winds over 119 km/h, also called a typhoon or cyclone.
panel.chemistry: Warm ocean water (above ~26°C) feeds rising moist air; the Earth's rotation organizes it into a spiraling system with a calm eye.
panel.mixology: Brings days of destructive wind, torrential rain, and dangerous storm surge along coasts.
panel.examples: On satellite, a huge spiral with a clear central eye; on the ground, prolonged extreme wind and rain in bands.
panel.flag: Hurricanes are life-threatening — heed evacuation orders, especially for storm-surge zones.

panel.origin: A massive electrical discharge between cloud and ground, or within clouds, hotter than the sun's surface.
panel.chemistry: Charge separates as ice and water collide in a storm cloud until the difference is great enough to leap as a giant spark.
panel.mixology: Always means a thunderstorm is present and you are at risk if outdoors — even before the rain arrives.
panel.examples: A brilliant branching flash followed by thunder; count the gap in seconds and divide by three for distance in km.
panel.flag: Lightning kills — if you can hear thunder, you are close enough to be struck; get indoors.

panel.origin: A widespread, fast-moving windstorm produced by a long line of intense thunderstorms.
panel.chemistry: A bow-shaped line of storms generates a continuous wall of strong straight-line winds that races across hundreds of kilometers.
panel.mixology: Causes tornado-like wind damage over a huge area without any rotation — a long, destructive blast of wind.
panel.examples: A long, advancing shelf cloud and a sudden, sustained roar of damaging straight-line wind across a wide region.
panel.flag: Derechos bring widespread destructive winds — treat warnings as seriously as a severe thunderstorm or tornado.

panel.origin: An arc of spectral color formed when sunlight passes through raindrops opposite the sun.
panel.chemistry: Light enters each raindrop, bends (refracts), reflects off the back, and bends again, splitting into colors at fixed angles.
panel.mixology: Tells you there's both rain and sunshine at once — the sun behind you, a shower ahead.
panel.examples: A colored arc opposite the sun, red on the outside, violet inside; a fainter secondary bow may appear above it.

panel.origin: A ring of light around the sun or moon, caused by ice crystals high in the atmosphere.
panel.chemistry: Light refracts through six-sided ice crystals in cirrostratus cloud, bending at a consistent 22° to form a ring.
panel.mixology: Often a sign of an approaching warm front and rain within a day — 'ring around the moon, rain soon.'
panel.examples: A bright circle at a fixed distance from the sun or moon, sometimes faintly rainbow-tinted, with thin high cloud.

panel.origin: Bright, often colorful spots that appear to the left and right of the sun, at the same height.
panel.chemistry: Flat, hexagonal ice crystals drifting horizontally refract sunlight to either side of the sun at 22°.
panel.mixology: Like halos, indicates high ice cloud and sometimes approaching weather. Also called mock suns or parhelia.
panel.examples: A pair of luminous, sometimes rainbow-edged patches flanking the sun, brightest when the sun is low.

panel.origin: Shimmering curtains of green, red, or purple light in the night sky near the poles — the Northern/Southern Lights.
panel.chemistry: Charged particles from the sun funnel along Earth's magnetic field and collide with gases high in the atmosphere, making them glow.
panel.mixology: Tells you a burst of solar activity (a geomagnetic storm) is hitting Earth's magnetic field.
panel.examples: Moving, glowing bands or rays of color in a dark polar sky, strongest after intense solar storms.

panel.origin: Sunbeams that appear to radiate from the sun through gaps in cloud — 'God rays.'
panel.chemistry: Sunlight streams through breaks in clouds and is scattered by dust and haze, making the individual beams visible.
panel.mixology: Mostly a beautiful optical effect; they mark broken cloud and scattered light rather than a forecast.
panel.examples: Distinct shafts of light fanning out from behind a cloud or below the horizon, especially near sunrise or sunset.

panel.origin: The leading edge of an advancing mass of cold air, pushing under and lifting the warm air ahead of it.
panel.chemistry: Dense cold air wedges beneath lighter warm air, forcing it up steeply and rapidly, often triggering towering clouds.
panel.mixology: Brings a sharp change: brief heavy showers or thunderstorms, then clearing skies, falling temperatures, and gusty winds.
panel.examples: A line of towering clouds and showers passing through, followed by cooler, drier, clearer air and a wind shift.

panel.origin: The leading edge of advancing warm air, gliding up and over the cooler air it's replacing.
panel.chemistry: Warm air rises gently over a wedge of cooler air, creating a long, sloping ramp of progressively lower cloud.
panel.mixology: Brings a slow sequence — high cirrus, then thickening cloud, then prolonged steady rain — followed by milder air.
panel.examples: A gradual lowering and thickening of cloud over a day, then steady rain, ending with warmer, more humid conditions.

panel.origin: A region where air sinks and spreads out at the surface, generally bringing calm, settled weather.
panel.chemistry: Descending air warms and dries, suppressing cloud formation. Winds circle it clockwise in the Northern Hemisphere.
panel.mixology: Usually means fair, dry, stable weather — sunny in summer, but clear cold nights and possible fog in winter.
panel.examples: On a map, an 'H' with widely spaced isobars; in the sky, clear or fair-weather conditions and light winds.

panel.origin: A region where air rises and converges, generally bringing clouds, wind, and unsettled weather.
panel.chemistry: Rising air cools and condenses into cloud and precipitation. Winds spiral inward, counterclockwise in the Northern Hemisphere.
panel.mixology: Means changeable, often wet and windy weather, and is where fronts and storms organize.
panel.examples: On a map, an 'L' with tightly packed isobars; in the sky, increasing cloud, wind, and rain.

panel.origin: A fast, narrow ribbon of wind high in the atmosphere that steers weather systems around the globe.
panel.chemistry: Strong temperature contrasts between air masses create a band of very high-speed winds near the top of the troposphere.
panel.mixology: Its position determines where storms track and whether you get warm or cold spells — the master switch of mid-latitude weather.
panel.examples: Invisible from the ground, but its influence shows as the path storms follow; pilots feel it as high-altitude wind.

HIGH CLOUDS.en: High clouds live above 6,000 m and are made entirely of ice crystals — that's why cirrus looks so wispy and why a milky cirrostratus sky throws a halo around the sun.
HIGH CLOUDS.es: High clouds live above 6,000 m and are made entirely of ice crystals — that's why cirrus looks so wispy and why a milky cirrostratus sky throws a halo around the sun.
HIGH CLOUDS.fr: High clouds live above 6,000 m and are made entirely of ice crystals — that's why cirrus looks so wispy and why a milky cirrostratus sky throws a halo around the sun.
HIGH CLOUDS.it: High clouds live above 6,000 m and are made entirely of ice crystals — that's why cirrus looks so wispy and why a milky cirrostratus sky throws a halo around the sun.

MID CLOUDS.en: Meteorologists use the prefix 'alto-' for mid-level clouds — so altostratus and altocumulus are simply the mid-height versions of stratus and cumulus.
MID CLOUDS.es: Meteorologists use the prefix 'alto-' for mid-level clouds — so altostratus and altocumulus are simply the mid-height versions of stratus and cumulus.
MID CLOUDS.fr: Meteorologists use the prefix 'alto-' for mid-level clouds — so altostratus and altocumulus are simply the mid-height versions of stratus and cumulus.
MID CLOUDS.it: Meteorologists use the prefix 'alto-' for mid-level clouds — so altostratus and altocumulus are simply the mid-height versions of stratus and cumulus.

LOW CLOUDS.en: Stratocumulus is the most common cloud on Earth, covering huge stretches of ocean — and plain stratus is really just fog that never reached the ground.
LOW CLOUDS.es: Stratocumulus is the most common cloud on Earth, covering huge stretches of ocean — and plain stratus is really just fog that never reached the ground.
LOW CLOUDS.fr: Stratocumulus is the most common cloud on Earth, covering huge stretches of ocean — and plain stratus is really just fog that never reached the ground.
LOW CLOUDS.it: Stratocumulus is the most common cloud on Earth, covering huge stretches of ocean — and plain stratus is really just fog that never reached the ground.

TOWERING CLOUDS.en: A single cumulonimbus can hold the energy of several atomic bombs and tower 12 km high, flattening into an anvil when it hits the stratosphere it can't punch through.
TOWERING CLOUDS.es: A single cumulonimbus can hold the energy of several atomic bombs and tower 12 km high, flattening into an anvil when it hits the stratosphere it can't punch through.
TOWERING CLOUDS.fr: A single cumulonimbus can hold the energy of several atomic bombs and tower 12 km high, flattening into an anvil when it hits the stratosphere it can't punch through.
TOWERING CLOUDS.it: A single cumulonimbus can hold the energy of several atomic bombs and tower 12 km high, flattening into an anvil when it hits the stratosphere it can't punch through.

PRECIPITATION.en: Whether you get rain, sleet, snow, or freezing rain often comes down to a layer of warm air only a few hundred meters thick somewhere above your head.
PRECIPITATION.es: Whether you get rain, sleet, snow, or freezing rain often comes down to a layer of warm air only a few hundred meters thick somewhere above your head.
PRECIPITATION.fr: Whether you get rain, sleet, snow, or freezing rain often comes down to a layer of warm air only a few hundred meters thick somewhere above your head.
PRECIPITATION.it: Whether you get rain, sleet, snow, or freezing rain often comes down to a layer of warm air only a few hundred meters thick somewhere above your head.

STORMS & SEVERE.en: Count the seconds between a lightning flash and its thunder and divide by three — that's roughly how many kilometers away the strike was.
STORMS & SEVERE.es: Count the seconds between a lightning flash and its thunder and divide by three — that's roughly how many kilometers away the strike was.
STORMS & SEVERE.fr: Count the seconds between a lightning flash and its thunder and divide by three — that's roughly how many kilometers away the strike was.
STORMS & SEVERE.it: Count the seconds between a lightning flash and its thunder and divide by three — that's roughly how many kilometers away the strike was.

SKY OPTICS.en: A rainbow is personal: every observer sees their own, because it's formed by the specific raindrops sitting at a 42° angle from the shadow of your own head.
SKY OPTICS.es: A rainbow is personal: every observer sees their own, because it's formed by the specific raindrops sitting at a 42° angle from the shadow of your own head.
SKY OPTICS.fr: A rainbow is personal: every observer sees their own, because it's formed by the specific raindrops sitting at a 42° angle from the shadow of your own head.
SKY OPTICS.it: A rainbow is personal: every observer sees their own, because it's formed by the specific raindrops sitting at a 42° angle from the shadow of your own head.

WEATHER SYSTEMS.en: The jet stream can blow over 400 km/h and is why flights from west to east are often faster — planes ride it like a river in the sky.
WEATHER SYSTEMS.es: The jet stream can blow over 400 km/h and is why flights from west to east are often faster — planes ride it like a river in the sky.
WEATHER SYSTEMS.fr: The jet stream can blow over 400 km/h and is why flights from west to east are often faster — planes ride it like a river in the sky.
WEATHER SYSTEMS.it: The jet stream can blow over 400 km/h and is why flights from west to east are often faster — planes ride it like a river in the sky.
